comments.author: @peterallman8474
comments.text:
I was fascinated with this, till about 5 minutes before the end when I started to feel cheated and frustrated.  All the talk about those tunnels, but no film footage. 

Then I realised neither had there been anything about the people, how they lived, shopped, socialised etc. 

I may return sometime for the last five minutes but for now it's comment and look for something else.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Fair critique, and specific enough to act on - promising the tunnels in narration and not delivering the footage is exactly the kind of gap worth fixing. We'll take a look.

comments.author: @mikenetherlin6912
comments.text: So if the whale meat is full of chemicals isn't the salmon that they raise and sell to everyone else also full of chemicals I think so.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Good logical follow-up - farmed salmon can carry contaminants too, though generally at lower levels than long-lived predators like pilot whales, which accumulate mercury and other toxins over decades at the top of the food chain. Different scale of concern, but not zero for either.

comments.author: @Phoenixesper1
comments.text:
"The whale meat... it is not sold, and no one profits from it."
Hate to drop a deuce in the punch bowl on this point, but the faroese do in fact sell whale meat, mainly to icelandic buyers, whos country has a mortiorium on commercial whale hunting, where as the faroe islands don't. Theres a reason the recent hunts have killed so many, because wealthy icelanders will pay an exorbitant price per pound for whale meat. They sell and ship multiple tons of whale meat to iceland each year through small private sales. In truth most faroese don't eat their share of whale, they sell it. So lets not gild the lily here, its not about survival or tradition, its a side hustle.

replies.author: @Phoenixesper1
replies.text: @WhereonEarth4K The trade market has existed for decades between iceland and faroe. Infact until very recently they also traded whale meat to norway, but recent regulations ended that route. Further whale meat tourism has also begun in recent years where wealthy elite from around the world travel to the faroes specifically to dine on rare whale meats. As an old saying goes, if you want to most out of a nickle, squeez it till the buffalo shits.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Appreciate the detailed pushback - if there's a real export market to Iceland as you describe, that would be a meaningful correction to the "not sold" framing in the narration. Worth verifying against current sources.

comments.author: @bigstyx
comments.text: The puffin population has decreased to a point where it is at extreme risk.  It’s not endangered, but it’s right next to being endangered.  I am aware that there are laws now that limit the taking of these birds, but it wasn’t managed as this video says.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Appreciate the correction — you're right that the population pressure is real even without formal endangered status, and it's worth being more precise about how recently and how well the harvest limits have actually been enforced.

replies.author: @NikhilChaudhariimbevda
replies.text: @WhereonEarth4Kohh. The video didn’t cover any of the energy or food sources hence the first thing that came to mind when I saw the waterfall was like hey why don’t they generate electricity using the river water

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: They actually do — hydropower is already a significant part of the Faroese energy mix, alongside some of the most ambitious offshore wind projects in the North Atlantic. The same geography that makes life hard there makes renewable energy surprisingly viable. 🙏

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: The Faroese imported almost all their timber — and before that, driftwood was genuinely precious, arriving on the tide from Siberian rivers. The treeless landscape made every plank a decision. Thank you for watching. 🌿

comments.author: @JazzLogic
comments.text:
This was so well done. From top to bottom. I was thoroughly engrossed in this. And I will say this, and if there is an argument about it okay. But this is like watching democracies ancestor. 
The the beginning of things. 

Perked up with the narrator said the Thing. 
My imagination took flight. Assemblies like these I start imagining the actual seen. There are no microphones. No polished marble halls. No air conditioning. No consultants. No PowerPoint presentations. It was just a bunch of weather beating people standing on a rocky peninsula arguing over sheep, fishing rights, inheritance disputes, and who let who's going to wander where. 😂😂😂

It was democracy as a necessity. 


When you have a small population in a harsh environment, endless feuds are expensive. 
You basically need every mechanism to stop every disagreement from turning in the bloodshed. 

So people gather. 

Talk. Argue. Threaten. Negotiate. Then everybody goes home. 

But at the end of the day, they get along. They survived. They thrive. Together. 

Superlatively done!

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: "Democracy as a necessity" — that framing reorders the entire political philosophy textbook. Thank you for that. 🌿

comments.author: @wondervista-90
comments.text: The Faroe Islands might be one of the few places where modern technology and ancient traditions still coexist without completely destroying each other. Do you think that's true, or is tourism slowly changing everything?

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That tension is one of the most honest things about the Faroe Islands — a place still in the middle of deciding what it wants to become. The fishing economy and the creeping infrastructure tell two very different stories at once. What in the film made you feel it could still hold?

comments.author: @eleonoraiggulden439
comments.text: Why don't posts about places like this tell us how they actually live, is there a grocery store, how do they import products they need - Home Depot / Ikea.  do they have internet? What did they do before internet. Do they have electricity? I think this is also important other than the land issues. I think other viewers also would like to see this. What about the tunnels under the sea, and the roundabout?

replies.author: @Archaism.1203
replies.text: Of course there is a grocery store and hotels to stay, what kind of questions are these? And of course they have to import tons of food, all of these things are basic common sensical things. You want to watch an episode of a vlogger that stays in these places if you want cost, specifics, and viability of living/moving there. Every video about the Faroe Islands isnt going to give you the answer to your every question. Do your own research. In an age of immense knowledge and information, we all of a sudden want people to do it all for us.

replies.author: @lindaandersenholmes8270
replies.text: @OutiLeppalathe lifestyle, the services, the schools etc. could be shown without invading anyone’s privacy IMO. It would be as interesting as this was. Fascinating and very interesting corner of the world. I don’t think it would bring an influx of people wanting to take up residence as it is an old society and a lifestyle most couldn’t adapt to. Limited work opportunities I would expect. Maybe the islanders don’t want tourism as they seem to do quite well with their salmon industrybthiugh. I don’t know. 🤷‍♀️ It would be interesting to know how they feel and what they think about these questions.
